items.question: Where does feature scaling belong in a feature-engineering pipeline?
items.answer: After feature construction, imputation, and categorical encoding, on the continuous numeric branch only, immediately before distance-, gradient-, or penalty-sensitive estimators. Fit the scaler inside the same Pipeline or ColumnTransformer on training data only. Canonical StandardScaler and MinMaxScaler formulas live in scaling and normalization—this topic owns placement and algorithm-sensitive include/skip rules.

items.question: Which algorithms need a scale branch on engineered numerics and which usually do not?
items.answer: Need a scale branch: k-NN, k-means, SVM with RBF kernels, Ridge/Lasso, neural nets, PCA on mixed engineered columns. Usually omit for pure tree or gradient boosting on engineered numerics because splits depend on order, not absolute scale—unless a later linear or distance step is added.

items.question: Should you StandardScaler one-hot encoded columns in ColumnTransformer?
items.answer: No. Keep categoricals in a separate branch with imputer and encoder only; apply StandardScaler to continuous engineered numerics in the numeric branch. Scaling binary indicators distorts 0/1 semantics and does not help distance or regularization.

items.question: How do you avoid leakage when scaling engineered features?
items.answer: Split data before any scaler fit; nest StandardScaler inside Pipeline with the model and call fit on X_train only. Use cross_val_score on the Pipeline object, not on pre-scaled full matrices. Serialize the fitted Pipeline for production so the same train-learned μ/σ transform engineered columns at inference.
